title: Remove sheet display_order from persistence model
description:
Problem / goal:
The backend sheets table still has a display_order column that preserves workbook.sheets load order. After explicit sheet frame zIndex was added for visual stacking, display_order no longer has a clear MVP product purpose and its name can be confused with z-order. Sheet identity should come from stable sheet IDs, not array position.

Implementation scope:
- Remove display_order from the backend persistence model and migrations in a forward-compatible way.
- Update WorkbookRepository save/load code so sheet persistence no longer writes or orders by display_order.
- Do not preserve a separate sheet ordering concept as product behavior.
- Update tests and code that identify sheets by array position when they should use sheet IDs or names instead.
- Keep any incidental SQL ordering only where needed for stable test output, and do not expose it as workbook semantics.

Out of scope:
- Do not change sheet zIndex behavior or frame stacking controls.
- Do not add a sheet navigator, sheet list, tabs, or user-controlled sheet ordering.
- Do not rename unrelated persistence fields.

Acceptance criteria:
- The persistence schema no longer contains or depends on display_order for sheets.
- Existing saved databases can migrate without losing sheets, cells, positions, dimensions, or zIndex.
- Sheet frame z-order remains controlled only by zIndex.
- Code and tests do not rely on workbook.sheets array position for sheet identity where a sheet ID/name should be used.
- Backend and frontend tests pass.

Plan references:
- plan/features/persistence.md
- plan/features/sheet-frames.md
- plan/mvp.task.md

Testing expectations:
Run make test and make compile. Include repository/migration coverage for databases created before the removal migration if feasible.

title: Improve multiline formula cell editing comfort
description:
Problem / goal:
The formula display bead sheetspace-sux changed the cell editor to a textarea so stored raw formulas with line breaks can round-trip, but the editor is still confined to the cell height. Multiline formulas should be comfortable to inspect and edit: text should be readable beyond a single grid cell, and long content should remain accessible without losing the exact stored raw formula text.

Implementation scope:
- Provide a cell editing UI for multiline raw cell content that can expand beyond the base cell size or use an overlay anchored to the active cell.
- Preserve raw formula text exactly, including casing, spacing, quoting, tabs, and line breaks.
- Keep evaluated formula results visible outside edit mode and raw formula text visible during edit mode.
- Define a maximum editor size appropriate for sheet frames and workspace zoom, then render internal scrollbars once content exceeds that size.
- Ensure the full raw text can be read and navigated inside the editor when the maximum size is reached.
- Preserve existing MVP commit and cancel behavior unless a separate product decision explicitly changes it: Enter commits, Escape cancels, blur or selection movement commits.
- Keep formula error cells selectable and editable with the same multiline editor behavior.
- Avoid breaking sheet frame dragging, grid selection, workspace pan and zoom, or formula recomputation after commit.

Out of scope:
- Rich formula editor features such as syntax highlighting, reference tokens, hover cards, autocomplete, or formula comments.
- A separate formula bar.
- Broad spreadsheet keyboard redesign.
- Changing how formulas parse or evaluate.

Acceptance criteria:
- Editing a stored multiline formula opens an editor that is larger than the base cell when needed.
- The editor has a documented maximum width and height and shows scrollbars, or equivalent scroll affordance, when content exceeds that maximum.
- Users can read and move through the entire raw formula text while editing, including lines beyond the visible area.
- Committing preserves the raw formula text exactly and updates the evaluated display result through existing recomputation.
- Cancelling restores the previous displayed result or error without mutating raw cell content.
- Formula error cells using multiline formulas remain selectable and editable.
- The editor remains visually anchored to the edited cell and does not resize rows, columns, or sheet frames unexpectedly.

Plan references:
- plan/features/grid-editing.md
- plan/features/formula-editor.md
- plan/features/formulas.md
- plan/mvp.task.md

Dependencies / blockers:
- Depends on sheetspace-sux because that bead established formula display versus raw edit behavior and introduced textarea-backed raw preservation.

Testing expectations:
Frontend tests should cover opening a multiline formula in the larger editor, preserving raw newline text on commit and cancel, scrollbar or max-size behavior where assertable in jsdom, editing a multiline formula error cell, and recomputing the visible result after a multiline formula is changed. Add a manual verification note for visual sizing and scrollbar behavior if jsdom cannot fully assert layout.

Human decisions required:
No decision is required for the scoped refinement if existing Enter-to-commit behavior is preserved and multiline content is entered through paste or existing persisted text. If keyboard insertion of new line breaks is added, create or resolve a separate question bead for the commit versus newline shortcut before implementation.
close_reason: Completed multiline formula editor comfort improvement; focused tests and compile passed; strict reviewer approved; full coverage timeout captured as sheetspace-8sl

title: Define and implement sheet frame z-order for overlapping frames
description:
Problem / goal:
When sheet frames overlap, the visual stacking order can be unclear or inconsistent. MVP scope now includes explicit z-order controls so users can keep overlapping sheets understandable without automatic focus or drag behavior changing their layout.

Implementation scope:
- Add deterministic z-order state to workbook sheet/frame state.
- New sheets stack above older sheets by default.
- Render frames using frame-level stacking so a whole frame sits above or below another whole frame, preventing controls, sticky headers, or scrollbars from visually interleaving across frames.
- Add explicit controls or commands to move the current sheet one level up, one level down, to the top, or to the bottom.
- Persist updated z-order through the normal workbook persistence path.
- Keep grid/body editing, selection, header dragging, and workspace pan/zoom usable.

Out of scope:
- Automatic bring-to-front on drag, select, focus, or edit.
- Resize behavior.
- Collapse, minimize, hide, grouping, layers panel, or layout automation.
- Broad workspace selection/focus redesign beyond what is needed for deterministic stacking and explicit order commands.

Acceptance criteria:
- New sheets are stacked above older sheets.
- Overlapping sheet frames have a clear and deterministic visual stacking order.
- A user can move a sheet one level up and one level down.
- A user can move a sheet to the top and to the bottom.
- Z-order changes affect only the intended sheet.
- Dragging, selecting, focusing, or editing a sheet does not implicitly bring it to the front.
- Frame-level stacking prevents controls, sticky headers, or scrollbars from visually interleaving between overlapping frames.
- Updated z-order is reflected in workbook state for persistence.
- Behavior remains compatible with header dragging, grid selection/editing, and workspace pan/zoom.

Plan references:
- plan/features/sheet-frames.md
- plan/mvp.task.md
- plan/features/workspace.md

Dependencies / blockers:
- Depends on sheetspace-cj6 because dragging makes overlapping frame order visible during normal use.

Testing expectations:
Frontend tests should cover initial new-sheet stacking, explicit up/down/top/bottom ordering, no implicit raise on drag/select/focus/edit, independent ordering changes, frame-level non-interleaving where assertable, and pan/zoom compatibility where feasible. Add a manual verification note for visual overlap if jsdom cannot fully assert it.

Human decisions required:
None; z-order behavior is recorded in plan/features/sheet-frames.md and plan/mvp.task.md.

title: Split cell editing App tests

Cell edit entry, commit, cancel, clear, Tab/Enter movement, and focus/blur commit behavior are currently buried in App.test.tsx. Move them into a dedicated App integration file, while keeping component-level editor keyboard callback tests in SheetGridCell tests.

title: Split oversized App.tsx into focused frontend modules
description:
Problem / goal:
frontend/src/App.tsx was split in the first pass, but the current UI architecture still has controller-level coupling and prop drilling: App owns workbook commands, Workspace receives persistence details, sheet-frame gestures mutate workbook state directly, and SheetGrid mixes rendering, keyboard interpretation, focus, editing, and display-value selection. Continue this bead as a second-pass refactor focused on DRY, SOLID boundaries, and React composition while preserving current product behavior.

Implementation scope:
- Keep this as refactor-only work. Do not change user-visible behavior, UX flows, formulas, persistence semantics, or visual design except where required by equivalent markup/component extraction.
- Introduce a central workbook controller/command boundary, likely a hook such as useWorkbookController and/or a reducer plus command object. Commands should express user intent: createSheet, renameSheet, appendRow, appendColumn, updateCellContent, moveSheetFrame, resizeSheetFrame, and changeSheetZOrder.
- Centralize the repeated optimistic-update plus autosave-enqueue plus revision-retry pattern behind that command boundary so components do not coordinate setWorkbook, getApiMethod, enqueueEdit, and runRevisionedEdit directly.
- Keep formula evaluation derived from workbook state, but expose display results through selectors/controller output rather than passing raw plumbing through unrelated layers.
- Extract workspace interaction ownership: viewport state, pan/zoom behavior, workspace coordinate conversion, workspace context-menu state, and create-at-position intent should live behind a workspace controller/hook instead of being mixed into render composition.
- Extract sheet-frame gesture ownership: drag/resize DOM pointer handling should translate into command calls and should not know API or autosave details.
- Split grid responsibilities so rendering, keyboard command interpretation, edit lifecycle, cell focus/scrolling, and display value selection are testable in isolation. Candidate pieces include GridHeaders, GridCell, CellEditor, useGridInteraction or useCellEditingController, and display-value selectors.
- Prefer composition and explicit typed interfaces over one large prop chain. Avoid introducing a broad global context unless it clearly improves dependency direction and rerender behavior.

Out of scope:
- New product features such as ranges, formula navigation, reference overlays, undo/redo, command palette, minimap, copy/paste, or formula editor improvements.
- Replacing React, Vite, Vitest, or the backend API.
- Adding an external state-management library unless there is a narrow, well-justified need.
- Broad visual restyling or accessibility behavior changes unrelated to preserving current semantics.
design:
Target dependency direction:
UI components -> interaction hooks/controllers -> typed workbook command interface -> workbook reducer/domain helpers -> persistence adapter.

Target composition shape:
AppShell handles startup loading/error and dependency injection only.
WorkbookController owns workbook state, formula snapshot, save status, optimistic local updates, queued autosaves, revision retry, and command methods.
WorkspaceController owns viewport, pan/zoom, workspace focus/menu state, and workspace-to-screen coordinate conversion.
WorkspaceLayout composes toolbar, surface, overlays, and dialogs.
WorkspaceToolbar renders controls and emits intent-level callbacks.
WorkspaceSurface renders the spatial plane and routes workspace-level pointer/wheel/context events.
SheetFrameLayer maps sheets to frame controllers/components in deterministic z-order.
SheetFrame renders frame chrome, title, resize handles, body slot, and menu affordance.
SheetFrameInteractionController translates drag/resize pointer events into workbook commands.
SheetGrid renders a sheet-local grid with focused subcomponents for headers, cells, and editor.
GridInteractionController or hooks own spreadsheet keyboard behavior, selection, edit start/commit/cancel, and focus restoration.
WorkbookDialogs own create/rename form state and validation messaging, but commit through workbook commands.

Concrete cleanup goals:
- Presentational components should not receive setWorkbook, getApiMethod, enqueueEdit, or runRevisionedEdit.
- Repeated save queue keys and API calls should be hidden behind commands.
- Components should receive domain-level props and callbacks, not infrastructure plumbing.
- The current App integration tests should remain the high-level behavioral contract and should not be loosened to make the refactor pass.
- New isolated tests should make each extracted controller/hook/component safe to change without depending only on the App integration suite.
acceptance_criteria:
- Existing user-visible behavior is preserved, including startup loading/error handling, sheet creation paths, rename validation, pan/zoom, frame move/resize, z-order commands, row/column append, cell selection/edit/commit/cancel, formula display while preserving raw formula text, autosave status, failed-save behavior, and revision-conflict retry.
- App.tsx becomes a thin shell/composition root and no longer directly implements sheet create/rename/append/z-order/cell command logic beyond provider wiring and startup state.
- A typed workbook command/controller boundary exists and covers sheet creation, sheet rename, row append, column append, cell raw-content update, frame move, frame resize, and z-order changes.
- Presentational workspace, frame, grid, toolbar, menu, and dialog components do not depend on persistence details such as workbookApi methods, enqueueEdit, runRevisionedEdit, or raw setWorkbook.
- Workspace viewport and pointer interactions are isolated behind focused hooks/controllers with unit coverage.
- Grid rendering is decomposed enough that cell/header/editor rendering and keyboard/edit behavior can be tested without mounting the whole App.
- Keep the existing App-level integration tests as the behavioral contract; update them only for harmless query/import changes required by moved modules, not by weakening assertions.
- Add focused unit tests for newly introduced controllers/hooks/components, especially workbook commands/autosave orchestration, workspace viewport behavior, sheet-frame drag/resize command translation, grid keyboard/edit lifecycle, and dialog validation/submit behavior where extracted.
- Relevant frontend checks pass at minimum: npm run test and npm run build from frontend, plus git diff --check. Run root checks if the implementation touches shared/root behavior.
- Dedicated reviewer subagent review is completed before closing the bead, and feedback is addressed or recorded.
notes: Reopened on 2026-05-20 per user direction. The previous completed split remains valid history; this continuation is a second-pass architecture refactor with stronger design and test expectations.
close_reason: Completed; frontend checks passed and dedicated reviewer subagent approved after required test coverage was added

title: Build formula parser for SUM, A1 references, and ranges
description:
Context:
The MVP formula slice treats content beginning with = as a formula and supports SUM with same-sheet and cross-sheet A1-style cell references and 2D ranges. Formula text is stored verbatim; parsing may normalize tokens only in memory.

What to do:
Implement a small parser that recognizes formula inputs, parses SUM calls case-insensitively, parses comma-separated arguments, supports individual references and rectangular ranges, and supports Excel-like cross-sheet references using visible sheet names such as `Inputs!A1` and `'Sales Q1'!A1:B3`. Report typed parse/name/reference errors without throwing through the UI. Do not add arithmetic operators, string literals, named ranges, percentages, or additional functions.

Acceptance criteria:
- Non-formula content is ignored by the parser path.
- Formula text remains verbatim in workbook state; parser normalization does not write back to the cell.
- `SUM`, `sum`, and mixed-case variants parse as the SUM function.
- Whitespace and newlines are allowed around separators, parentheses, commas, colons, references, and after `=`, while malformed address tokens such as `A 1` remain invalid.
- Arguments can be same-sheet A1 references, same-sheet ranges, cross-sheet references, and cross-sheet ranges.
- Quoted sheet-name references parse for names with spaces or punctuation.
- Unsupported functions produce `#NAME!`; invalid syntax produces `#PARSE!`; unresolved references are represented for evaluator/display as `#REF!`.

Test expectations:
Unit tests cover valid MVP SUM examples, mixed-case function names, verbatim raw formula retention, whitespace/newline tolerance, invalid formulas, unsupported functions, invalid references, unquoted cross-sheet references, quoted cross-sheet references, and malformed quote/address cases.

Likely files or areas to inspect:
frontend/src, plan/features/formulas.md, plan/mvp.task.md

Dependencies/blockers:
Blocked by addressing and sheet-name lookup helpers from the MVP model bead.
